surface.name: Deploy the official Node.js sample to Cedar
surface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Deploy the official Node.js sample to Cedar. The runtime router has no default, but the current Node.js getting-started guide provides a complete vendor-sample route with explicit build, release, deploy, and browser signals. Prior surface before policy commit: Heroku runtime and getting-started guide not selected.

alternatives_considered.name: Node.js Cedar sample deployment
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a complete official candidate, but the general Getting Started page presents it alongside eight other language routes.

alternatives_considered.name: Heroku Fir with Node.js
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Fir availability depends on Private Spaces and adds an enterprise entitlement choice.

alternatives_considered.name: Deploy an existing application
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Existing code adds framework and repository-specific preparation instead of the vendor sample path.

entry_point.developer_intent: Deploy and open a first Heroku application without a selected runtime language or source application.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has arrived after discovery.
Runtime language, sample versus existing code, Cedar versus entitled Fir, and local operating system are not selected.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: The Heroku Node.js getting-started guide ends when the deployed official sample site loads at its herokuapp.com URL.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: The official sample site loads at its `herokuapp.com` URL.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The official sample site loads at its `herokuapp.com` URL.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The official sample site loads at its `herokuapp.com` URL.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: Selected under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the committed official candidate route.

prerequisites.requirement: Select a supported runtime and vendor sample or existing codebase.
Creating apps, deploying code, and using dynos require a verified Heroku account. Verification normally requires adding an accepted payment card in Account Settings, Billing, Add Credit Card.
Git and the Heroku CLI are shared prerequisites; the selected guide adds its runtime, package manager, framework, and dependency metadata.
The account payment method pays for used resources; card verification can create a $1 temporary preauthorization visible for up to seven days.
Deploying and running dynos counts toward usage; the Node.js tutorial recommends Eco where available and otherwise uses Basic by default.

primary_path.action: Choose the Heroku runtime guide and whether to deploy its vendor sample or an existing application.
details: Official choices include Node.js, .NET, Ruby, Java, PHP, Python, Go, Scala, and Clojure.
Specialized Rails, Heroku Connect, Windows, Cedar, and entitled Fir routes can add further choices.
primary_path.input: Developer language, framework, repository state, operating system, account entitlement, and desired runtime stack.
primary_path.success_signal: No shared deploy command exists at this router.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The path cannot be atomic until runtime and source are selected.

primary_path.action: Verify the Heroku account before creating the app or using dynos by opening Account Settings, Billing, and selecting Add Credit Card.
details: Heroku can also redirect the developer to the card page when a gated CLI or web action is attempted.
The payment method is retained to pay for resource usage.
primary_path.input: Heroku account and accepted credit or debit card.
primary_path.output: Verified Heroku account with a payment method on file.
primary_path.success_signal: The account is verified and app creation, deployment, and dyno actions are unlocked.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The verification may create a $1 temporary preauthorization that can remain visible for up to seven days; an unsupported card blocks verification.

primary_path.action: Follow the selected tutorial through local tool installation, CLI authentication, source preparation, app creation, remote build and release, and browser verification.
details: The Node.js candidate is fully enumerated in the branch below.
Other languages share the platform concepts but use different sample repositories, dependency files, buildpacks, and process commands.
primary_path.input: Selected runtime, verified account, local toolchain, source repository, and plan state.
primary_path.output: Running Heroku application at its generated URL.
primary_path.success_signal: The selected tutorial's build and release complete and the application loads in its documented verification interface.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Authentication, plan or usage limits, runtime dependency errors, buildpack detection, process declaration, build failure, release failure, or application startup failure vary by guide.

primary_path.action: Create or sign in to the Heroku account required by the Node.js guide.
details: The account must be accessible before verification and CLI login.
primary_path.success_signal: The Dashboard and Account Settings open.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Account or login recovery must finish first.

primary_path.action: Open Account Settings, Billing, select Add Credit Card, and complete account verification.
details: Verification is required before app creation, deployment, and dynos.
A temporary $1 preauthorization can remain visible for up to seven days.
primary_path.success_signal: App and dyno actions are unlocked.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: An unsupported card blocks verification; a permitted collaborator can instead use an app owned by a verified or Enterprise account.

primary_path.action: Install Git, a supported Node.js runtime with npm, and the operating-system-specific Heroku CLI.
details: All four tools are used by the sample route.
primary_path.input: Supported local machine and install permission.
primary_path.success_signal: Git, Node.js, npm, and Heroku CLI run in the terminal.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A missing tool stops the documented commands.

primary_path.action: Run `heroku login`, complete browser authentication, and return to the terminal.
details: The CLI reports the authenticated account email.
primary_path.success_signal: The CLI confirms the account email.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Browser login, account access, or network failure blocks app creation.

primary_path.action: Clone `https://github.com/heroku/nodejs-getting-started.git` and enter the repository directory.
details: This selects Heroku's sample instead of an existing codebase.
primary_path.success_signal: The sample files are present in the current directory.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Git, network, or filesystem failure blocks the clone.

details: The command creates the app, generated URL, and `heroku` Git remote on Cedar.
primary_path.input: Authenticated CLI, verified account, and sample repository.
primary_path.output: Heroku app, URL, and Git remote.
primary_path.success_signal: The command prints the created URL and remote.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Verification, entitlement, API, or stack errors prevent app creation; Fir requires its separate route.

primary_path.action: Run `git push heroku main` and wait for the remote build and release.
details: Heroku detects Node.js, installs runtime and dependencies, builds, discovers the web process, compresses, releases, and verifies.
primary_path.output: Remote build, release, and deploy verification.
primary_path.success_signal: Output reports `Build succeeded!`, `Released`, the URL, and `Verifying deploy... done`.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Buildpack detection, dependencies, build, process, release, plan, or startup errors require correction and another push.

primary_path.action: Run `heroku open` or visit the generated URL.
details: The browser result is the guide's first user-facing verification.
primary_path.success_signal: The official sample site loads at its `herokuapp.com` URL.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A blank, crashed, or unreachable app requires logs or startup correction before retrying.

branches.condition: The developer has no accepted card but is a collaborator or team member on an app owned by a verified or Enterprise account.
branches.path: Use the owner organization's verified app context instead of verifying a separate personal owner account, subject to the assigned collaborator or team permissions.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The personal-card gate can be avoided only when another verified or Enterprise account owns the app; the developer does not gain independent app ownership.

branches.condition: Node.js on Cedar with the official sample is selected.
branches.path: Install Git, Node.js, npm, and the OS-specific Heroku CLI; run `heroku login` and complete browser authentication until the CLI reports the account email; clone `https://github.com/heroku/nodejs-getting-started.git` and enter the directory; run `heroku create --stack heroku-26` to create an app, URL, and Git remote; run `git push heroku main`; wait while Heroku detects Node.js, installs the runtime and dependencies, builds, discovers the web process, compresses, releases, and verifies the deploy; confirm `Build succeeded!`, `Released`, the URL, and `Verifying deploy... done`; then visit the URL or run `heroku open` and verify that the sample site loads.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The Heroku-provided Node.js sample is deployed and externally visible in the browser.

branches.condition: Another official language guide is selected.
branches.path: Install the selected language runtime and package tools plus Git and Heroku CLI, authenticate, clone that guide's official sample, follow its dependency and process-file instructions, create the app on the documented stack, push the source, wait for the corresponding buildpack to install and build dependencies, verify release output, ensure the web dyno is running where the guide requires it, then open the generated URL and inspect the sample behavior.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The external application result is similar, while prerequisites, files, commands, build logs, optional database, and local verification are runtime-specific.

branches.condition: Existing application code is selected instead of a vendor sample.
branches.path: Leave the language sample tutorial and use Preparing a Codebase for Heroku Deployment plus the selected buildpack and framework documentation to declare dependencies, executable process types, configuration, backing services, and build requirements before creating and deploying the app.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The journey becomes repository-specific and can add database, config-var, migration, asset, and production-process gates.

branches.condition: The account is entitled to Heroku Fir and that stack is selected.
branches.path: Use the Fir-specific language guide and Private Spaces requirements rather than silently applying Cedar commands.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Entitlement, stack, networking, build, and application configuration can differ.

friction_gates.description: Runtime language, framework, sample versus existing code, stack, and operating system must be selected.
App creation, deployment, and dyno use require account verification through an accepted payment method.
Adding the payment card can create a $1 temporary preauthorization visible for up to seven days, and the method is used for subsequent resource charges.
Git, Heroku CLI, and runtime-specific local tools must be installed.
Browser-mediated CLI login is required after account verification.
Dyno usage counts toward account usage; the Node.js guide recommends Eco where available and otherwise defaults to Basic.
Runtime dependency files, process declaration, buildpack detection, and app configuration vary by language and framework.
Remote build, dependency installation, release, and deploy verification must finish before the application can be opened.
Successful build output alone is followed by opening the generated URL and confirming the running application.

sources.title: Getting Started on Heroku with Node.js
evidence_supported: Clone, app creation, build, release, deploy verification, and browser result

sources.title: Account Verification
sections_used: How to Verify and Pay for Resources Used on a Heroku Account
evidence_supported: Mandatory verification before app creation, deployment, and dynos
Account Settings, Billing, Add Credit Card path
$1 temporary preauthorization for up to seven days
Payment method pays for used resources

uncertainties.question: Which Heroku runtime, framework, source application, and stack should define first success?
uncertainties.impact: The choice changes local dependencies, source, buildpack, process declaration, optional services, deploy errors, and tutorial pages.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Heroku's official Getting Started router presents multiple language and specialized guides without a universal default.

excluded_after_success: Logs, scaling, dependencies, and local runtime explanation
Add-ons and databases unless the selected guide makes them pre-boundary
Production hardening, custom domains, pipelines, review apps, and CI/CD
